text:
The regulatory environment for digital assets (“crypto”) aside - the U.S. SEC has a poor history of providing modern American businesses with clear and navigable reporting guidelines and disclosure frameworks, without prohibitively expensive legal support from securities lawyers. 

We (citizens) should want more public transparency from U.S. based companies, not less! The current regulatory environment disincentivizes small and medium-sized businesses from being more proactively publicly accountable.

There should be a regulatory sandbox co-built as a coalition with public regulators that enable projects wanting to take a proactive approach to public disclosure. The U.K. has had an equivalent sandbox for years, and the U.S. has so far fumbled to create any meaningful collaboration in the decentralized finance world with good faith actors. 

The poor faith, regulation by enforcement agenda currently employed by the SEC is detrimental to the U.S. economy at large, global financial innovation, and the spirit of public accountability.

text:
The "food system" in the U.S. looks something like this....

production =
agriculture ->
processing ->
global distribution ->
local distribution ->
retail ->
consumption = YOU

At each phase of this "supply chain" there is a company.

Some of these companies are equitable co-ops, others are profit-maximizing shareholder corporations, margins vary.

Different layers of these chains receive banking services and capital support from different financial institutions, some use credit unions and regional banks, while others use shareholder owned national banks. 

Each company has a product, service, or mission...

And here's where things get interesting.

When a company in this chain is a co-op, the people involved actually own and govern it. Wild concept, right?

Take farmer co-ops. Instead of selling their crops to whatever giant corporation sets the price (take it or leave it), farmers band together. Suddenly they're not just price-takers but price-negotiators. The power dynamic shifts.

Some quick examples to consider:
Land O'Lakes? Actually a farmer-owned co-op! Not a corporate giant. Ocean Spray? Yep, owned by cranberry and grapefruit farmers. Your local food co-op grocery store? Owned by the shoppers who buy their kale and kombucha there.

The co-op difference shows up in how money moves through the system too. In a typical corporation, profits flow upward to distant shareholders who probably couldn't identify the company's products in a lineup.

In a co-op? Profits circle back to the people who use the business. The farmers get better prices. The workers get better wages. The shoppers get better products or end-of-year rebates. Money stays in the community instead of jetting off to Wall Street.

Where Co-ops Thrive (and Where They Don't)

Co-ops pop up all over the food chain, but not evenly. They're like mushrooms - appearing abundantly in some places, notably absent in others.

They're strongest at the farm level (think dairy co-ops, organic grain co-ops), the consumer level (your neighborhood food co-op), and specialty products (those fancy fair-trade chocolate co-ops).

But the middle of the chain? That's corporate territory. The massive processing plants, the cross-country trucking networks, the warehousing systems - these capital-intensive businesses remain largely investor-owned.

This creates a bottleneck. Farmer co-ops and consumer co-ops find themselves squeezing through corporate-controlled middle sections of the supply chain, where their values (and their margins) can get compromised.

The Numbers Tell a Story

Co-ops aren't just hippie grocery stores in college towns. They're economic powerhouses. Those farmer co-ops move over $200 billion in goods annually, credit unions pump billions into the food system every year, and worker-owned food businesses are growing faster than traditional businesses in many regions.

Still, co-ops represent just a fraction of our food economy. Cargill, JBS, Tyson, and Nestlé aren't shaking in their boots... yet.

What Would a More Co-op Food System Look Like?

Imagine if more of the supply chain operated as co-ops. What changes? Farmers could earn living wages instead of scraping by while middlemen profit, food processing workers could own their facilities instead of being treated as disposable, regional food networks could strengthen (reducing those crazy 1,500-mile salads), grocery prices could better reflect actual costs instead of corporate markup, and communities could keep more food dollars circulating locally.

It's not about destroying capitalism or forcing everyone into communes. I'm an MBA, not a marxist.

It's about rebalancing who benefits from our food system and who has a voice in how it works. 

Every time you choose a co-op product or shop at a co-op business, you're voting for a food system where power and profit are distributed more widely. Where the people who grow, prepare, deliver, and eat the food have a meaningful say in how things operate.

Not a bad outcome for simply choosing where to buy your bananas, right?

text:
The "social media ecosystem" in the U.S. looks something like this...

creation = 
content creators -> 
platform technology -> 
attention algorithms -> 
advertising engines -> 
data harvesting -> 
consumption = YOU

At each phase of this "digital chain" there is a company.

Almost none of these companies are equitable co-ops, most are profit-maximizing surveillance corporations, margins are astronomical.

Different layers of these chains receive venture capital and support from different financial institutions, almost all use shareholder owned national banks or private equity. Almost none use credit unions or community development financial institutions.

Each company has a product, service, or mission...
And in most cases, that product is actually YOU.

When a company in this chain is privately owned, the algorithms, data policies, and content moderation decisions get made behind closed doors by people who never have to face their users. Strange arrangement, isn't it?

Take Facebook (sorry, "Meta"). When they decide to change their algorithm, billions of people's information environments shift overnight. No vote, no public input, no community governance. Just Mark and his team deciding what's best for their quarterly numbers.

What if social media platforms were actually owned and governed by their users? What if the people posting, liking, sharing, and scrolling had a say in how the technology operated?

Some brave experiments are already happening: Mastodon? A decentralized network of independently operated servers, no central authority. Diaspora? User-owned data that you can take with you across the internet. Platform cooperatives? Emerging models where users literally own shares in the platforms they use.

The co-op difference would transform social media economics too. In typical social platforms, your attention gets monetized, your data gets sold, and profits flow upward to distant venture capitalists who view users as commodities.

In a cooperative social platform? The value generated could flow back to the community, finance better content moderation, fund quality journalism, or even provide dividends to users who contribute the most value.

Where Co-op Social Media Could Thrive

The digital landscape is ripe for cooperative alternatives, especially at certain pressure points in the system: content moderation that respects community standards, data ownership that centers user privacy, algorithm design that optimizes for user wellbeing instead of engagement, and financing models that don't require exponential growth.

The biggest hurdle isn't technology - it's the network effect. Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, TikTok didn't win because they had the best technology, they won because everyone's friends were already there. This creates a massive barrier to entry for new platforms, cooperative or otherwise.

But cracks are appearing in the armor. Users are increasingly frustrated with surveillance capitalism, content moderation decisions seem arbitrary and inconsistent, and trust in tech giants continues to erode.

The Numbers Tell a Story

The "Big Tech" social media landscape is dominated by a handful of corporations with trillion-dollar valuations, billions of users, and unprecedented power over our public discourse. Meanwhile, cooperative alternatives remain tiny, with user bases measured in thousands or millions rather than billions.

And yet, the cooperative internet movement grows. Decentralized platforms, user-owned data structures, blockchain-based governance models, and community-operated servers are all expanding their footprints in the digital ecosystem.

What Would Cooperative Social Media Look Like?

Imagine logging into a platform where the terms of service were actually written by and voted on by users, where content moderation decisions were made through representative governance, where algorithms were designed to maximize community wellbeing rather than engagement, where privacy was a fundamental design principle rather than an afterthought, and where the economic value generated was shared among those who created it.

It's not about destroying social media or forcing everyone onto obscure platforms with clunky interfaces. I'm a design engineer, not a digital anarchist.

It's about rebalancing who benefits from our digital commons and who has a voice in how it functions.

Every time you choose a cooperative digital platform, you're voting for a social media ecosystem where power and control are distributed more equitably. Where the people who create, curate, moderate, and consume the content have a real stake in how the system operates.

Not a bad outcome for simply choosing where to share your cat photos, right?

creation = 
content creators -> 
platform technology -> 
attention algorithms -> 
advertising engines -> 
data harvesting -> 
consumption = YOU

Almost none of these companies are equitable co-ops, most are profit-maximizing surveillance corporations, margins are astronomical.

Each company has a product, service, or mission...
